identity.title: Kaolin, Clay, and Ceramic and Refractory Minerals Mining

lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, providing repeat outsourced mine-site development, extraction, or basic beneficiation of kaolin, ball clay, common clay, bentonite, fuller's earth, fire clay, and ceramic or refractory minerals for external mineral owners or industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Mine owners selling their own mineral output as a commodity or proprietary product, captive operations, mineral-rights holding vehicles, post-beneficiation calcining or manufacturing, support activities classified outside 212323, and one-off earthmoving without recurring customer programs.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Multi-period B2B operating or toll-beneficiation arrangements priced by ton, campaign, capacity, or fixed operating fee, with repeat extraction, washing, screening, grinding, sizing, quality-control, and load-out obligations.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat outsourced extraction and beneficiation because the NAICS is primarily mineral-owning product production, while the fixed lens requires an outsourced service. Public sources did not establish a defensible LMM population for that subset.

a.bridge: BLS occupations for the broader mining-except-oil-and-gas subsector, dominated by equipment operators, extraction supervisors, and truck drivers, are combined with ILO broad-occupation GenAI exposure to infer the wage-weighted current opportunity for the 212323 service lens. This can overstate exposure where work is mostly field-based and understate it where geology, dispatch, permitting, quality, and commercial functions carry higher wages.
a.rationale: AI can assist geological interpretation, dispatch, maintenance knowledge, quality documentation, environmental reporting, scheduling, procurement, and administration, but excavation, hauling, processing, sampling, and equipment care remain physical.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupation roster and task-time study from representative clay mines and toll-beneficiation plants, including their current autonomous-equipment and software baseline, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix covers NAICS 212, not 212323 or a verified contract-operator subset.
ILO exposure measures technical task potential rather than current unautomated work.
The frozen labor ratio is measured at ancestor 21232 and may not match the narrowed lens.

rho.rationale: Planning, document search, dispatch support, preventive-maintenance analysis, quality reporting, and back-office workflows can be implemented within five years, while remote sites, weak data infrastructure, safety controls, environmental compliance, and equipment integration slow adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Mine-level pilots reporting adoption, uptime, recovery or yield, safety incidents, reporting time, headcount avoidance, integration cost, and worker acceptance would materially change the range.
caveats: No 212323-specific AI implementation study was found.
Implementation varies materially with mine scale, connectivity, fleet age, and plant instrumentation.

e.rationale: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, and public sources do not distinguish independent outsourced operators from mineral owners selling their own production. Neither the denominator nor eligible numerator can be bounded without re-estimating the missing dataset input.
e.change_evidence: A verified establishment-to-firm roster with ownership of mineral rights, EBITDA, customer contracts, toll or operating revenue, captive status, and NAICS classification is required.
caveats: Industry counts in USGS cover clay and shale companies broadly, not the screened service model or LMM band.
Contract support operations may be classified in NAICS 213115 rather than 212323.

s5.rationale: No defensible eligible LMM population was identified, so a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability cannot be estimated without applying unrelated ownership statistics to an unknown set of firms.
s5.change_evidence: After establishing the eligible roster, target owner ages, mineral-right ownership, family succession plans, sponsor ownership, broker mandates, and five-year control-transaction history would be required.
caveats: Mine asset sales are not necessarily transfers of eligible operating firms.
Large mineral-company divestitures do not proxy owner succession in an unidentified LMM population.

q.rationale: Fixed-fee or fixed-rate-per-ton operating contracts could retain productivity gains until repricing, but escalation formulas, open-book costing, customer concentration, commodity competition, and renewal negotiations would force substantial sharing.
q.change_evidence: Actual tolling and mine-operation contracts, escalation clauses, renewal cadence, open-book provisions, bid histories, and customer concentration would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No representative public contract-term dataset for 212323 outsourced operators was found.
Mineral-product sale pricing should not be confused with service billing under the narrowed lens.

d5.bridge: USGS production volumes and end-use mix for all U.S. clays are translated into year-five demand for outsourced extraction and basic beneficiation in 212323. This can overstate the lens if producers keep operations captive and understate it if industrial customers increasingly outsource variable capacity or specialist beneficiation.
d5.rationale: USGS estimates total U.S. clay production at 26.0 million tons in 2025 versus 25.7 million in 2021, with kaolin rising from 4.39 million to 4.8 million tons. Diverse uses in fillers, ceramics, tile, absorbents, brick, aggregate, cement, drilling mud, and refractories support stability but remain construction- and industrial-cycle exposed.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year mine plans, reserve depletion, customer volume forecasts, toll-beneficiation bookings, construction and ceramics demand, and constant-quality tonnage by mineral would materially change the range.
caveats: USGS clay totals include mineral categories and operators outside the exact service lens.
Product tonnage is not a direct measure of outsourced operating-service demand.
Quality differences make simple tonnage an imperfect constant-quality measure.

o.rationale: Clay extraction and beneficiation remain physical, permitted operations requiring accountable operators for safety, environmental compliance, quality, maintenance, and delivery. AI can change workflows, but customers could self-operate or substitute minerals, which reduces the retained outsourced quantity.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on customer insourcing, autonomous-mine operating models, mineral substitution, synthetic alternatives, mine closures, and tolling-contract renewals would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate separates AI displacement from conventional autonomous equipment.
Loss through customer insourcing is more plausible than software-only replacement.

narrative.executive_view: The clay-mining category has stable physical demand and some AI-addressable planning and administrative work, but public evidence does not establish an LMM population of outsourced operators inside 212323. The dominant model is mineral production, not recurring service.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve geological interpretation, dispatch, maintenance, quality reporting, permitting support, scheduling, and administrative workflows; excavation, hauling, beneficiation, sampling, and compliance still require field operators and equipment.
narrative.value_capture: Contract operators could retain gains during fixed-fee or per-ton pricing periods, although open-book terms, escalation formulas, renewals, and concentrated customers would share much of the benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen LMM count is explicitly missing, and USGS company counts do not distinguish service operators from mineral owners or establish EBITDA. Eligibility and transfer probability therefore remain missing.
narrative.demand_durability: USGS data show broadly stable total clay volumes and modest kaolin growth, supported by diverse industrial and construction uses. That supports mineral demand but does not prove growth in outsourced mine operation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are nonexistence of a scalable target pool, misclassification into mining-support NAICS, reserve depletion, permitting and reclamation liabilities, construction cyclicality, customer concentration, freight costs, mineral substitution, and captive customer operation.
narrative.principal_driver: Diversified, recurring physical demand for clay minerals across fillers, ceramics, absorbents, construction materials, drilling, and refractories.
narrative.principal_weakness: The required outsourced-service target population and its transferability cannot be established from available evidence.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022
sources.supports: The 212323 industry title and activity boundary for kaolin, clay, and ceramic and refractory mineral mining and beneficiation.

sources.title: Mining (except Oil and Gas): NAICS 212
sources.supports: Broader mining occupation mix, including equipment operators, extraction supervisors, and heavy truck drivers.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A global analysis of potential effects on job quantity and quality
sources.supports: Task-level GenAI exposure by broad occupation, with clerical work much more exposed than production and operator groups.

sources.title: Mineral Commodity Summaries 2026: Clays
sources.supports: U.S. clay production, value, company count, mineral-specific volumes, end uses, exports, employment, and recent five-year trends.

sources.title: Recent labor productivity, unit labor costs, and related data, 2025
sources.supports: Recent broader nonmetallic-mineral-mining output and productivity context.
